task: I’m trying to sort out a shipment that arrived short and the supplier is pushing back on the invoice. Can you help me figure out the cleanest way to document the discrepancy and what I should ask for before I agree to pay anything else?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the cleanest approach is to document the shortage like a mini audit trail and keep the supplier focused on facts, not opinions.

## What to document
Create a short discrepancy record with:

- **Purchase order number**
- **Supplier invoice number**
- **Delivery date**
- **Carrier / tracking / bill of lading number**
- **Item description and SKU**
- **Ordered quantity vs. received quantity**
- **Difference / shortage**
- **Condition on receipt**  
  - sealed cartons?
  - damaged packaging?
  - evidence of tampering?
- **Who received it**
- **Photos / video**
- **Count method used**
  - full count, carton count, weight check, etc.
- **Any warehouse receiving notes or signed delivery exception**

If possible, attach:
- signed delivery receipt with exceptions noted
- packing slip
- invoice
- purchase order
- photos of the shipment and labels
- any carrier paperwork

## What to ask the supplier for
Before agreeing to pay anything else, ask for:

1. **Their proof of shipment**
   - packing list
   - pallet/carton count
   - weight details
   - bill of lading or carrier manifest

2. **Their proof of quantity shipped**
   - itemized ship confirmation
   - serial numbers or lot numbers if applicable

3. **Their explanation for the mismatch**
   - whether it was a short ship, backorder, split shipment, or invoicing error

4. **A corrected invoice or credit memo**
   - if they billed for more than was received

5. **Written confirmation of how they want to resolve it**
   - replacement shipment
   - partial credit
   - debit memo
   - revised invoice after investigation

## Best practice on payment
If the shortage is unresolved, don’t just pay the full disputed amount unless:
- you have a written agreement that the remaining balance is not waived, or
- you’ve received a corrected invoice, credit, or replacement terms in writing.

If you need to pay the undisputed portion, pay **only the amount you agree is correct** and clearly label it:
- **“Partial payment – invoice disputed for shortage”**

## Simple dispute note you can send
> We received shipment [PO/invoice number] on [date] and found a shortage of [quantity] for [item/SKU]. Our receiving record shows [quantity received] vs. [quantity invoiced/ordered]. Please provide your shipping documentation and advise whether you will issue a corrected invoice, credit memo, or replacement for the missing quantity. We will remit the undisputed amount once the discrepancy is resolved in writing.

## If you want, I can also draft:
- a formal discrepancy email,
- a receiving report template,
- or a one-page dispute log you can use internally.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is close. But I’d tighten it up more before I send anything.

What I actually need is the cleanest version that protects me if they try to turn around later and say I accepted the shortage anyway.

So give me:
- a short discrepancy log format I can use internally
- the exact wording to put on the invoice or remittance if I pay the undisputed part
- the minimum documents I should require from the supplier before any extra payment
- and one blunt email template that doesn’t sound weak

Also, if there’s a better way to handle it when the shipment was signed for without counting every unit at delivery, I want that too. That’s usually where they try to wiggle out.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. The key is to create a record that shows **you noticed the shortage promptly, disputed it expressly, and did not accept the missing quantity by paying or signing without reservation**.

## 1) Short discrepancy log format
Use something simple and factual. Keep it to one page if possible.

**Shipment Discrepancy Log**

- **Date received:**  
- **Supplier:**  
- **PO number:**  
- **Invoice number:**  
- **Delivery / BOL / tracking number:**  
- **Item / SKU:**  
- **Ordered qty:**  
- **Invoiced qty:**  
- **Received qty:**  
- **Shortage qty:**  
- **Receiving method:**  
  - full count / carton count / weight check / visual check only
- **Condition on receipt:**  
  - sealed / damaged / tampered / no visible issue
- **Who received it:**  
- **Who counted it:**  
- **When discrepancy discovered:**  
- **How discovered:**  
- **Photos / attachments:**  
- **Notes:**  
  - “Shipment accepted subject to count verification”  
  - “Shortage disputed with supplier on [date/time]”  
  - “No waiver of rights or acceptance of shortage”

**Best line to include in the notes:**  
> Receipt of shipment does not constitute acceptance of quantity, and all rights are reserved pending final count and written resolution of discrepancy.

That sentence helps a lot if they later claim you waived the issue.

---

## 2) Exact wording for invoice or remittance when paying the undisputed part
Use something direct and unambiguous.

### On the check memo / remittance advice / payment note:
> **Partial payment only — invoice disputed for shortage of [item/SKU] in the amount of [qty]. Payment does not waive claim for missing goods. Rights reserved.**

If space is tight, use:
> **Partial payment. Disputed shortage remains open. No waiver of rights.**

If you can put a note on the remittance advice or ACH remittance:
> **This payment covers only the undisputed portion of Invoice #[number]. Buyer disputes shortage and reserves all rights regarding the balance.**

If you’re sending a check, I’d also attach a short cover note repeating the same point.

---

## 3) Minimum documents to require from the supplier before any extra payment
Ask for these before you pay anything beyond the undisputed amount:

1. **Proof of shipment**
   - packing slip
   - bill of lading / carrier manifest
   - shipment confirmation

2. **Proof of quantity shipped**
   - carton/pallet count
   - itemized pick list
   - weight details
   - serial/lot numbers if relevant

3. **Their written explanation**
   - short ship
   - split shipment
   - backorder
   - invoicing error
   - carrier loss

4. **Corrective document**
   - corrected invoice, or
   - credit memo, or
   - written replacement commitment

5. **Written resolution**
   - what they are doing
   - by when
   - whether they agree the shortage is unresolved

If they can’t produce proof of shipment quantity, that’s a strong sign you should not pay the disputed balance yet.

---

## 4) Blunt email template
Here’s a firm version that stays professional and doesn’t sound apologetic.

**Subject:** Shipment shortage dispute – PO #[PO number] / Invoice #[invoice number]

> [Supplier name],  
>   
> We received the shipment for PO #[PO number] on [date] and our receiving record shows a shortage of [qty] units of [item/SKU]. We are disputing the invoiced amount to the extent it exceeds the quantity actually received.  
>   
> Please send the following so we can close this out:  
> - packing slip and shipment confirmation  
> - bill of lading / carrier paperwork  
> - itemized proof of quantity shipped  
> - your explanation for the shortage  
> - corrected invoice, credit memo, or written resolution proposal  
>   
> We will pay the undisputed amount only. Any payment made now is not an acceptance of the shortage and does not waive our rights regarding the missing quantity.  
>   
> Please respond in writing by [date].  
>   
> Regards,  
> [Name]

If you want it a little harder:
> Until we receive the requested documentation, the disputed balance remains on hold.

---

## 5) If the shipment was signed for without counting every unit
This is where you want to be careful, but it’s still manageable.

### Best way to handle it
If the delivery was signed for without a full count, your position should be:

- the signature acknowledged **receipt of shipment**, not acceptance of final quantity
- the shortage was discovered during subsequent receiving/count verification
- the discrepancy was reported promptly
- you reserved rights immediately

### What to write in the receiving record
Use a note like:
> Shipment received subject to count verification. Signature acknowledges delivery only and does not confirm quantity received.

If the driver asked for a clean signature and you had not counted yet, you want to avoid language like “received in full” or “complete.”

### If you already signed without a reservation
You still may be able to dispute it, but your evidence matters more:
- internal count records
- photos
- packing slip
- warehouse logs
- weight checks
- timestamped discrepancy notice sent right away

The faster you notify them, the better. If the shortage was discovered later, document:
- when the count was done
- who did it
- how it was counted
- why the shortage couldn’t have been seen at the dock

### Strongest practical phrasing for future deliveries
On the delivery receipt, if allowed:
> **Received subject to count and inspection. Shortages or damage to be reported upon verification.**

If the carrier or driver won’t accept that, at least keep your internal receiving note consistent with it.

---

## 6) One practical rule
Don’t mix up these two things:
- **accepting delivery**
- **accepting quantity**

You can accept delivery and still dispute quantity later, as long as your records clearly show that.

If you want, I can turn this into:
1. a one-page internal discrepancy log template, and  
2. a more aggressive version of the email if they keep stonewalling.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly there. The wording is usable, but I’d want it a little tighter around the part they’ll try to twist later.

A couple things I’d still want:
- a cleaner internal log line for "accepted subject to count" that doesn’t sound sloppy
- a stronger remittance note that blocks any claim of full settlement
- and one version for when the driver already got a clean signature, because that’s the weak spot

Also, I’d want the minimum docs split into **must-have before any balance is paid** vs **nice to have**, because suppliers love dumping half a packet and calling it "proof."

If you can tighten those pieces, that’s the version I’d actually use.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter version.

## 1) Cleaner internal log line
Use this instead of “accepted subject to count”:

> **Delivery accepted for receipt purposes only; quantity remains subject to verification and no acceptance of full quantity is implied.**

Shorter version:
> **Receipt acknowledged only; quantity pending verification.**

If you want it even more explicit:
> **Signed for delivery only, not for confirmed quantity.**

---

## 2) Stronger remittance / invoice note
This is the version I’d use on a check stub, remittance advice, or payment memo:

> **Partial payment only. Buyer disputes the shortage on PO #[PO] / Invoice #[invoice]. This payment is for the undisputed amount only and does not constitute accord, satisfaction, waiver, or acceptance of the disputed balance. All rights reserved.**

If you need a shorter version:
> **Partial payment only. Disputed shortage remains open. No waiver, no full settlement, all rights reserved.**

If there’s room, add:
> **Any endorsement or deposit of this payment does not release the disputed claim.**

That last line is useful if they later try to argue the payment settled everything.

---

## 3) If the driver already got a clean signature
This is the weak spot, so the goal is to separate **delivery acknowledgment** from **quantity acceptance** as fast as possible.

### Use this internal note:
> **Shipment signed for on delivery without unit count verification. Signature confirms receipt of shipment only and does not confirm quantity received. Shortage discovered upon subsequent count and disputed immediately.**

### If you’re documenting it after the fact, include:
- date/time of delivery
- who signed
- whether cartons/pallets were counted or only visually checked
- when the full count happened
- when the shortage was found
- when the supplier was notified

### Best follow-up line to the supplier:
> **Although the shipment was signed for on delivery, the signature was a receipt acknowledgment only and did not confirm quantity. The shortage was identified during receiving verification and is disputed.**

That phrasing matters because it closes off the “you signed, so you accepted it” argument.

---

## 4) Minimum documents: must-have vs nice-to-have

### Must-have before any disputed balance is paid
These are the non-negotiables:

1. **Their proof of quantity shipped**
   - packing slip
   - itemized ship confirmation
   - carton/pallet count
   - bill of lading or carrier manifest

2. **Their written explanation**
   - short ship
   - split shipment
   - backorder
   - invoicing error
   - carrier loss

3. **A corrective document or resolution in writing**
   - corrected invoice
   - credit memo
   - replacement commitment
   - written agreement on how the shortage will be resolved

4. **A direct match to the disputed line**
   - PO number
   - invoice number
   - SKU/item
   - quantity short

If they can’t give you those, I would not pay the balance.

### Nice-to-have, but not required before payment
These help your file, but I wouldn’t let them delay you forever:

- photos of pallets/cartons
- weight tickets
- serial or lot numbers
- carrier delivery exception notes
- warehouse receiving photos
- signed delivery receipt with exceptions
- email from the driver or warehouse
- internal count sheet
- CCTV stills, if available

---

## 5) Tight discrepancy log format
Here’s a cleaner internal format you can drop into a spreadsheet or one-page log:

**Shipment Discrepancy Log**

- **Date received:**  
- **Supplier:**  
- **PO number:**  
- **Invoice number:**  
- **Delivery / BOL / tracking number:**  
- **Item / SKU:**  
- **Ordered qty:**  
- **Invoiced qty:**  
- **Received qty:**  
- **Shortage qty:**  
- **Receiving status:**  
  - delivery accepted for receipt only / quantity pending verification  
- **Condition on receipt:**  
  - sealed / damaged / tampered / no visible issue  
- **Received by:**  
- **Counted by:**  
- **Count method:**  
- **Date shortage discovered:**  
- **Date supplier notified:**  
- **Attachments:**  
- **Notes:**  
  - “No acceptance of full quantity implied.”  
  - “Disputed immediately upon verification.”  
  - “Rights reserved.”

---

## 6) One blunt email template
Here’s a tighter version that doesn’t sound soft:

**Subject:** Disputed shortage – PO #[PO] / Invoice #[invoice]

> [Supplier name],  
>   
> We received the shipment for PO #[PO] on [date]. The shipment was signed for as received, but quantity was not verified at delivery. Upon receiving verification, we found a shortage of [qty] units of [item/SKU].  
>   
> We dispute the invoiced amount to the extent it exceeds the quantity actually received.  
>   
> Before any disputed balance is paid, send the following:  
> - packing slip and shipment confirmation  
> - bill of lading / carrier paperwork  
> - itemized proof of quantity shipped  
> - your written explanation for the shortage  
> - corrected invoice, credit memo, or written resolution  
>   
> We will pay only the undisputed amount. No payment made now waives the shortage claim or constitutes acceptance of the missing quantity.  
>   
> Please respond in writing by [date].  
>   
> Regards,  
> [Name]

---

## 7) Best practical move if you already signed cleanly
If this happens again, the best line at delivery is:

> **Received subject to count and inspection.**

If they won’t accept that on the carrier receipt, at least make sure your internal receiving record says:
> **Receipt acknowledged only; quantity pending verification.**

That way, even if the delivery signature was clean, your records still show you did **not** accept the quantity as final.

If you want, I can turn all of this into a single copy-paste package:
- internal log template
- remittance note
- supplier email
- receiving note for clean-signature deliveries

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the version I’d actually use.

The split between must-have and nice-to-have is what I wanted, and the remittance language is tighter now. The clean-signature wording is decent too — that’s usually where they try to play games.

The only thing I’d maybe tweak on my end is keeping the internal note short enough that nobody has to interpret it twice:

> Receipt acknowledged only; quantity pending verification.

That’s clean. Hard to argue with later.

If I need the full copy-paste package later, I’ll use it.
